module: Implement continuous integration and continuous delivery (CICD)

questions.question: What are the two components of lifecycle management in Fabric that together implement CI/CD?

options.A: Git integration and Deployment pipelines
options.B: GitHub and Azure DevOps
options.C: REST APIs and Power BI Desktop
options.D: Workspaces and Items

questions.explanation: The module states that managing the lifecycle of Fabric items using CI/CD has two parts: integration (implemented using Git) and deployment (implemented using Fabric deployment pipelines). Automation is handled via REST APIs.

questions.question: Which version control systems does Microsoft Fabric natively support for Git integration?

options.A: GitLab and Bitbucket
options.B: GitHub and Azure DevOps
options.C: GitHub and AWS CodeCommit
options.D: Azure DevOps and GitLab

questions.explanation: The module explicitly states that GitHub and Azure DevOps are the version control systems supported in Fabric.

questions.question: At what level is Git integration configured in Microsoft Fabric?

options.A: Item level
options.B: Tenant level
options.C: Workspace level
options.D: Capacity level

questions.explanation: The module explicitly states: 'Integration with version control is at the workspace level in Fabric. You can version items you develop within a workspace.'

questions.question: What is continuous integration (CI)?

options.A: Automatically deploying code to production once all tests pass
options.B: Frequently committing code to a shared branch, validated by automated build and testing
options.C: Deploying to a staging environment for more testing before production release
options.D: Moving content between development, test, and production stages using pipelines

questions.explanation: CI involves frequently committing code to a shared code branch in a version control system, where changes are validated by a build process and automated testing, identifying conflicts earlier in the development process.

questions.question: What does the 'Changes' option in the Fabric Source control window do?

options.A: Pulls new commits from the Git branch into the workspace
options.B: Creates a new branch from the current workspace
options.C: Synchronizes workspace changes to the connected Git branch
options.D: Merges branches in the Git repository

questions.explanation: When you make workspace changes, you synchronize them with the Git branch using the 'Changes' selection in the Source control window.

questions.question: What does the 'Updates' option in the Fabric Source control window do?

options.A: Pushes local workspace changes to the Git branch
options.B: Synchronizes new commits from the Git branch into the workspace
options.C: Creates a pull request in Git
options.D: Deletes stale branches from the remote repository

questions.explanation: When new commits are made in the Git branch, you synchronize them with your workspace using the 'Updates' selection in the Source control window.

questions.question: What does the deployment process do when content is deployed between pipeline stages?

options.A: Moves content from the source stage and deletes it from the original
options.B: Creates a new pipeline stage automatically
options.C: Clones (copies) all workspace content into the target stage
options.D: Merges content between two stages into a new combined stage

questions.explanation: The deployment process copies (clones) all of the workspace content into the target stage, typically from development to test and from test to production.

questions.question: Which two tools are listed in the module as client tools for isolated development outside the Fabric web interface?

options.A: Power BI Desktop and VS Code
options.B: Excel and Power Query Editor
options.C: GitHub Desktop and Azure Data Studio
options.D: SQL Server Management Studio and Postman

questions.explanation: The module lists Power BI Desktop (for reports and semantic models) and VS Code (for Notebooks) as client tools for isolated development outside the Fabric web interface.

questions.question: What is the difference between continuous delivery and continuous deployment?

options.A: Continuous delivery requires manual approval before production; continuous deployment automatically releases to production after tests pass
options.B: Continuous delivery deploys to development; continuous deployment deploys to test
options.C: Continuous delivery uses Git; continuous deployment uses deployment pipelines
options.D: There is no difference; they are synonyms in the context of Fabric

questions.explanation: Continuous delivery deploys code to a staging environment for more automated testing before release to production. Continuous deployment automatically releases updates into production through structured stages once they pass automated tests.

questions.question: Where is the Git status column visible after connecting a Fabric workspace to Git?

options.A: Only in the Fabric admin portal
options.B: In the workspace view, showing the sync state of items compared to the remote branch
options.C: Only in the deployment pipeline stage view
options.D: Only in the Source control REST API response

questions.explanation: After connecting to a repository, the workspace shows a Git status column indicating the sync state of items in the workspace compared to the items in the remote branch.

questions.question: What is the recommended best practice for development work to avoid affecting other shared workspace users?

options.A: Make changes directly in the shared production workspace quickly
options.B: Develop in an isolated workspace connected to a personal branch
options.C: Delete the shared workspace before making changes
options.D: Commit changes directly to the main branch without a PR

questions.explanation: The module states that a best practice is for developers to work in an isolated workspace outside of a shared, live workspace, using their own protected workspace connected to a personal branch.

questions.question: When combining deployment pipelines with Git integration, which workspace is commonly connected to Git to avoid sync conflicts in higher environments?

options.A: Only the Test workspace
options.B: Only the Production workspace
options.C: Only the Development workspace
options.D: All three pipeline stage workspaces equally

questions.explanation: The module describes a common approach where only the Development workspace is connected to Git, while deployment pipelines handle promotion to Test and Production. This avoids potential Git synchronization conflicts when deploying content across multiple stages.

questions.question: What can Fabric CI/CD REST APIs be used to automate?

options.A: Only creating new Fabric workspaces
options.B: Committing workspace changes, updating from Git, checking Git status, and deploying pipeline stages
options.C: Only managing user permissions in the tenant
options.D: Only monitoring pipeline run status

questions.explanation: The module lists Fabric CI/CD REST API operations: commit changes to Git, update workspace from Git, check Git status, list deployment pipeline stage items, and deploy stage content.

questions.question: What is the purpose of creating a dedicated feature branch in Git during development?

options.A: To increase the number of automated deployments per day
options.B: To allow multiple developers to work on a feature without affecting the main branch
options.C: To permanently reduce the size of the Git repository
options.D: To automatically deploy changes directly to production

questions.explanation: The module states that feature development in a dedicated branch makes it easy for multiple developers to work on a feature without affecting the main branch.

questions.question: What does a Pull Request (PR) accomplish in the Git branching workflow?

options.A: Pushes code directly to the production environment bypassing all tests
options.B: Creates a new isolated workspace automatically for the developer
options.C: Requests to merge changes from a feature branch into the main branch
options.D: Runs automated tests directly in the Fabric deployment pipeline

questions.explanation: A PR is created in Git to pull changes from a developer's isolated branch into the main branch, enabling code review and validation before the merge is completed.

questions.question: A data engineering team has 5 developers all working in the same Fabric workspace simultaneously. Changes made by one developer are breaking other developers' work. What should the team adopt to solve this problem?

options.A: Grant all developers read-only access to the shared workspace
options.B: Have each developer use an isolated workspace connected to a personal branch, with pull requests to merge into the main branch
options.C: Delete and recreate the workspace after each developer finishes their work
options.D: Allow only one developer to work in the workspace at a time using a sign-up schedule

questions.explanation: The recommended practice is for each developer to work in an isolated workspace connected to their own branch, then submit pull requests to merge changes into the main branch, avoiding interference between developers.

questions.question: A developer has made changes in their isolated branch and is ready to incorporate their code into the team's shared codebase. What is the correct next step according to Fabric Git integration practices?

options.A: Directly edit the main branch items in the shared Fabric workspace
options.B: Use the Deploy button in the deployment pipeline to push to production
options.C: Create a Pull Request (PR) in Git to merge the isolated branch into the main branch
options.D: Delete the main branch and rename the feature branch to main

questions.explanation: After committing to an isolated branch, the developer creates a PR in Git to merge those changes into the main branch. The main branch is updated once the PR is merged.

questions.question: After multiple developers merge their changes into the main Git branch, a team lead opens the shared development workspace and sees a notification. What does this notification indicate?

options.A: The workspace has been deleted by another developer
options.B: There are new commits in the Git branch that need to be synchronized into the workspace
options.C: A deployment pipeline stage has failed
options.D: The workspace license has expired

questions.explanation: When new commits are merged into the main branch, opening the shared workspace prompts users to synchronize the new content from Git with the shared workspace.

questions.question: A team needs code to release automatically to production once it passes all automated tests, with no manual intervention. Which CI/CD concept describes this requirement?

options.A: Continuous integration
options.B: Continuous delivery
options.C: Continuous deployment
options.D: Version control branching

questions.explanation: Continuous deployment is a process that automatically releases updates into production environments through structured deployment stages, once they pass automated tests — no manual intervention required.

questions.question: A Fabric workspace is connected to a Git repository. A developer commits new notebook changes to the remote branch. A teammate wants to reflect these changes in the workspace. What must they do in the Source control interface?

options.A: Select 'Changes' to push local changes to the branch
options.B: Select 'Updates' to pull commits from Git into the workspace
options.C: Create a new deployment pipeline for the updated notebook
options.D: Recreate the workspace from scratch to pick up the changes

questions.explanation: The 'Updates' selection in the Source control window synchronizes new commits made in the Git branch into the workspace.

questions.question: An organization wants to combine Git version control with deployment pipelines but wants to avoid Git synchronization conflicts when promoting content across pipeline stages. What approach does the module recommend?

options.A: Connect all three pipeline stages (Dev, Test, Production) to separate Git branches
options.B: Connect only the Production workspace to Git for final version tracking
options.C: Connect only the Development workspace to Git and use deployment pipelines to promote to Test and Production
options.D: Use separate Git repositories for each pipeline stage

questions.explanation: The module recommends connecting only the Development workspace to Git, while deployment pipelines handle promotion to Test and Production. This avoids potential Git synchronization conflicts when deploying content across multiple stages.

questions.question: A data engineer develops notebooks in VS Code and wants to test them in Fabric before issuing a pull request. What is the correct sequence of steps?

options.A: Clone the repo locally, push changes to remote, connect isolated branch to a workspace, then issue a PR to merge into main
options.B: Directly edit notebooks in the production workspace and monitor for errors
options.C: Use only the Fabric web interface for all notebook development
options.D: Deploy directly to production using the Fabric REST API without testing

questions.explanation: The module describes this workflow for client tool development: clone repo locally, develop, push to remote repository, connect the isolated branch to a separate workspace to test, then issue a PR to merge into the main branch.

questions.question: An admin wants to automate the process of committing all workspace changes to the connected Git branch on a nightly schedule. Which Fabric feature enables this?

options.A: Deployment pipelines scheduled trigger
options.B: Fabric REST APIs
options.C: Workspace Monitoring
options.D: Power BI Desktop scheduled publish

questions.explanation: Fabric CI/CD REST APIs can be used to automate the commit process programmatically, including committing workspace changes to the connected remote branch on any schedule.

questions.question: A developer needs to know which Fabric items in the workspace have local changes not yet committed to Git. Which tool or feature provides this information?

options.A: The Fabric admin portal under Tenant settings
options.B: The Git status API or the Source control window's Git status column in the workspace
options.C: The deployment pipeline overview showing stage differences
options.D: The Capacity Metrics app activity log

questions.explanation: The Git status API and the workspace's Git status column both show which items differ between the workspace and the remote Git branch, identifying uncommitted local changes and incoming remote updates.

questions.question: A Fabric workspace contains a data pipeline, a lakehouse, and a semantic model. When the team selects 'Deploy' from Development to Test in the deployment pipeline, what exactly gets copied?

options.A: Only newly added items since the last deployment
options.B: All workspace content is copied to the Test stage
options.C: Only the data pipeline since it was the most recently modified item
options.D: A new Git branch is created for the Test stage automatically

questions.explanation: The deployment process copies all of the workspace content into the target stage, not just newly added or recently modified items.

questions.question: A team has created a deployment pipeline but hasn't assigned any workspaces to the stages. What must happen before they can deploy content?

options.A: All content must first be committed to a Git repository
options.B: A workspace must be assigned to each pipeline stage
options.C: The deployment pipeline must be connected to Azure DevOps
options.D: All items in the workspace must be endorsed as certified

questions.explanation: The pipeline creation process requires assigning a workspace to each stage and selecting the green check mark before content can be deployed between stages.

questions.question: A company wants to use Fabric REST APIs to automate deployment from the Development stage to the Test stage of a deployment pipeline. Which REST API operation does this require?

options.A: Git status API to check for pending changes
options.B: Deploy Stage Content API
options.C: Update workspace from Git API
options.D: Commit changes to Git API

questions.explanation: The 'Deploy Stage Content' REST API deploys items from the specified stage of the specified deployment pipeline to the target stage.

questions.question: A Fabric workspace admin sees the source control icon showing the number '5.' What does this indicate?

options.A: There are 5 users currently connected to the workspace
options.B: 5 items differ between the workspace and the Git repository
options.C: 5 automated tests have failed in the deployment pipeline
options.D: The workspace has 5 deployment pipeline stages configured

questions.explanation: The source control icon shows the number of items that are different between the workspace and the repository, indicating uncommitted changes or incoming updates.

questions.question: A developer in the Fabric web interface wants to start working on a new feature without affecting the shared development workspace connected to the main branch. What should they do directly from the Fabric interface?

options.A: Clone the main branch in Git and create an entirely new repository
options.B: Select 'Branch out to new workspace' in Source control to create an isolated branch and associated workspace
options.C: Delete the shared workspace and recreate it for their personal use
options.D: Grant themselves Admin access to bypass shared workspace restrictions

questions.explanation: Developers using the Fabric web interface can select 'Source control' then 'Branch out to new workspace' to create an isolated branch and a new workspace that syncs with that branch.

questions.question: A Fabric admin wants to view all items currently in the Test stage of a deployment pipeline before initiating another deployment. Which Fabric REST API operation should they use?

options.A: Git status API
options.B: Commit changes to Git API
options.C: List Deployment Pipeline Stage Items API
options.D: Deploy Stage Content API

questions.explanation: The 'List Deployment Pipeline Stage Items' REST API returns the supported items from the workspace assigned to the specified stage of the specified deployment pipeline.

questions.question: After deploying content from Development to Test, the team wants to verify all three pipeline stages are synchronized. What visual indicator in the deployment pipeline UI confirms this?

options.A: A red exclamation mark in the stage box indicating review is needed
options.B: A checkmark in each deployment stage box indicating items exist in all stages and are synchronized
options.C: A blue arrow indicating a pending deployment is queued
options.D: A lock icon indicating the stage content is frozen

questions.explanation: The module describes a checkmark in the deployment stage box as the indicator that a data pipeline item exists in all staging environments and that the Fabric stages are synchronized.

questions.question: An organization's CI/CD process uses GitHub. What serves as the central location for storing and managing Fabric items in this setup?

options.A: The Fabric admin portal under Tenant settings
options.B: The OneLake storage account
options.C: The GitHub repository
options.D: A dedicated development-stage workspace

questions.explanation: The Git repository (GitHub in this case) is the central location for storing and managing items when using Git integration in Fabric. Setting up the repository is the first step in implementing Git integration.

questions.question: A team wants multiple developers to collaborate on a single feature without affecting the main code branch. What development strategy should they adopt?

options.A: All developers commit directly to the main branch in small increments
options.B: Create a single dedicated feature branch; all feature developers commit to that branch
options.C: Each developer directly modifies items in the production workspace
options.D: Only one developer works on a feature at a time in the shared workspace

questions.explanation: The module recommends that feature development take place in a dedicated branch instead of the main branch, making it easy for multiple developers to work on a feature without affecting the main branch.

questions.question: A developer made changes in VS Code locally and is now ready to test them in Fabric. After pushing changes to the remote branch, what must be configured to test in the Fabric environment?

options.A: Deploy the changes directly to the production pipeline stage
options.B: Connect the isolated feature branch to a separate Fabric workspace for testing
options.C: Merge directly into main without testing to save time
options.D: Enable workspace monitoring to auto-detect the new notebook

questions.explanation: The module states that after pushing changes to the remote repository when using client tools, the developer should test by connecting their isolated branch to a separate workspace before issuing a PR.

questions.question: A Fabric CI/CD automation script needs to first commit all workspace changes to Git, and then only if successful, deploy from Development to Test. Which two REST API operations are combined?

options.A: Git status check then List pipeline stage items
options.B: Commit to Git then Deploy Stage Content
options.C: Update workspace from Git then Commit to Git
options.D: List pipeline items then Update workspace from Git

questions.explanation: The script first calls the commitToGit API (commits workspace changes to the connected remote branch) and if successful, calls the Deploy Stage Content API (deploys items between pipeline stages).

questions.question:
Contoso has a Fabric data engineering team with 6 developers building a lakehouse-based analytics platform. The team needs: (1) developers to work in isolation without disrupting each other, (2) code history and revert capability, (3) content deployed through Development → Test → Production environments, and (4) nightly automated deployments from Development to Test. They use Azure DevOps as their version control system. Which combination of tools and steps provides a complete solution?

Note: This question tests the integration of all CI/CD concepts covered in this module.

options.A: Use only deployment pipelines; all developers commit directly to the main branch; manually trigger all deployments; no API automation
options.B: Connect the Development workspace to Azure DevOps; each developer branches out to an isolated workspace via 'Branch out to new workspace'; developers commit to their branch and issue PRs to main; use a three-stage deployment pipeline (Dev → Test → Production) for promotion; automate nightly Dev-to-Test deployments using the 'Deploy Stage Content' Fabric REST API
options.C: Use only Git with no deployment pipelines; developers commit directly to the production branch; disable REST API automation for security; rely on manual copy-paste between environments
options.D: Connect all three pipeline stages to Git for full synchronization; use only Git to handle deployments between environments; disable deployment pipelines to avoid redundancy

questions.explanation: This scenario requires all components from the module: (1) Isolated workspaces via 'Branch out to new workspace' for developer isolation; (2) Azure DevOps for version control and code history; (3) A three-stage deployment pipeline for environment promotion; (4) The 'Deploy Stage Content' REST API for automated nightly deployments. Option B is the complete module-aligned solution addressing all four requirements.

questions.question:
A data engineer writes the following Python code to interact with the Fabric REST API:

import requests

token = 'Bearer <access_token>'
workspace_id = 'abc-123'
url = f'https://api.fabric.microsoft.com/v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}/git/commitToGit'

payload = {
    'mode': 'All',
    'comment': 'Nightly automated commit'
}

headers = {'Authorization': token, 'Content-Type': 'application/json'}
response = requests.post(url, json=payload, headers=headers)
print(response.status_code)

What does this code do?

options.A: Updates the Fabric workspace with the latest commits from the Git branch
options.B: Checks the current Git status of all items in the workspace
options.C: Commits all workspace changes to the connected remote Git branch
options.D: Deploys content from Development to Test in a deployment pipeline

questions.explanation: The URL endpoint 'git/commitToGit' with a POST method and 'mode': 'All' commits all workspace changes to the connected remote Git branch. This maps to the 'Commit the changes made in the workspace to the connected remote branch' operation described in the module.

options.A: Commits local workspace changes to the Git repository
options.B: Updates the Fabric workspace with commits pushed to the connected Git branch
options.C: Lists all items in a specific deployment pipeline stage
options.D: Creates a new Git branch from the current workspace state

questions.explanation: The 'git/updateFromGit' POST endpoint updates the workspace with commits from the connected Git branch. This corresponds to the 'Update the workspace with commits pushed to the connected branch' operation in the module.

questions.question:
A developer writes the following code to check CI/CD status:

import requests

token = 'Bearer <access_token>'
workspace_id = 'abc-123'
url = f'https://api.fabric.microsoft.com/v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}/git/status'

headers = {'Authorization': token}
response = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
data = response.json()

for item in data.get('changes', []):
    print(f"Item: {item['itemMetadata']['displayName']}, Remote: {item['remoteChange']}, Workspace: {item['workspaceChange']}")

What information does this code provide?

options.A: The deployment history of content promoted between pipeline stages
options.B: Which items have incoming changes from Git and which items have uncommitted local workspace changes
options.C: The list of all workspaces in the Fabric tenant
options.D: The current users connected to the workspace at this moment

questions.explanation: The 'git/status' GET endpoint returns the Git status of items in the workspace, including which items have incoming changes from the remote branch (remoteChange) and which have local uncommitted changes (workspaceChange). This maps to the Git status API described in the module.

questions.question:
The following code automates a deployment pipeline operation:

import requests

token = 'Bearer <access_token>'
pipeline_id = 'pipeline-xyz'

url = f'https://api.fabric.microsoft.com/v1/pipelines/{pipeline_id}/deployAllItems'

payload = {
    'sourceStageOrder': 0,
    'targetStageOrder': 1,
    'note': 'Automated nightly deployment'
}

headers = {'Authorization': token, 'Content-Type': 'application/json'}
response = requests.post(url, json=payload, headers=headers)
print(f'Deployment triggered: {response.status_code}')

What does this code accomplish?

options.A: Commits workspace changes to the connected Git branch
options.B: Deploys all items from the Development stage (order 0) to the Test stage (order 1) of the deployment pipeline
options.C: Updates the Development workspace from the Git repository
options.D: Creates a new deployment pipeline with three stages

questions.explanation: This code calls the Deploy Stage Content REST API to deploy items from Development (stageOrder 0) to Test (stageOrder 1) in the specified deployment pipeline, implementing the automated deployment operation described in the module.

questions.question:
A team uses this Python snippet to retrieve pipeline information:

import requests

token = 'Bearer <access_token>'
pipeline_id = 'pipeline-xyz'
stage_order = 1  # Test stage

url = f'https://api.fabric.microsoft.com/v1/pipelines/{pipeline_id}/stages/{stage_order}/items'
headers = {'Authorization': token}

response = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
items = response.json().get('value', [])

for item in items:
    print(f"Item: {item['itemDisplayName']}, Type: {item['itemType']}")

What does this code return?

options.A: The users assigned to the Test stage workspace
options.B: The supported items in the workspace assigned to the Test stage of the deployment pipeline
options.C: The Git commit history for the Test workspace
options.D: The list of all deployment pipelines in the tenant

questions.explanation: This code calls the 'List Deployment Pipeline Stage Items' REST API, which returns the supported items from the workspace assigned to the specified stage (Test, order 1) of the deployment pipeline.

options.A: {'Authorization': 'Token access_token_here'}
options.B: {'Authorization': 'Bearer access_token_here'}
options.C: {'X-API-Key': 'access_token_here'}
options.D: {'Auth': 'Bearer access_token_here'}

questions.explanation: Fabric REST APIs use standard Azure REST API authentication with Bearer tokens. The correct authorization header format is 'Bearer <access_token>', as shown in the module's REST API examples.

options.A: Both items exist in the remote Git branch and are fully synchronized with the workspace
options.B: 'SalesNotebook' has local changes not committed to Git; 'CustomerPipeline' has a new commit in Git not yet pulled into the workspace
options.C: Both items need to be deleted from the workspace and recreated
options.D: The workspace has been disconnected from the Git repository

questions.explanation: 'workspaceChange': 'Modified' for SalesNotebook means it has local changes not yet committed to Git (use 'Changes' to sync). 'remoteChange': 'Added' for CustomerPipeline means a new version exists in Git not yet pulled into the workspace (use 'Updates' to sync).

questions.question:
Consider this Python automation script:

def run_cicd_cycle(workspace_id, pipeline_id):
    # Step 1
    status_resp = requests.get(f'{base}/workspaces/{workspace_id}/git/status', headers=headers)
    has_changes = any(c['workspaceChange'] != 'None' for c in status_resp.json().get('changes', []))

    # Step 2
    if has_changes:
        commit_resp = requests.post(f'{base}/workspaces/{workspace_id}/git/commitToGit',
                                    json={'mode': 'All', 'comment': 'Auto commit'}, headers=headers)

    # Step 3
    deploy_resp = requests.post(f'{base}/pipelines/{pipeline_id}/deployAllItems',
                                json={'sourceStageOrder': 0, 'targetStageOrder': 1}, headers=headers)
    return deploy_resp.status_code

What does this function do in sequence?

options.A: Updates workspace from Git → Checks deployment status → Lists pipeline items
options.B: Checks Git status for uncommitted changes → Commits any workspace changes to Git → Deploys from Development to Test
options.C: Commits to Git → Checks if Test stage is synchronized → Updates workspace from Git
options.D: Lists pipeline items → Commits to Git → Creates a new isolated workspace

questions.explanation: Step 1 calls the Git status API to check for uncommitted workspace changes. Step 2 conditionally calls commitToGit if changes exist. Step 3 calls the Deploy Stage Content API to deploy from Development (stage 0) to Test (stage 1). This represents a full CI/CD automation loop using three REST API operations from the module.

questions.question:
An automation engineer writes a script that retrieves Git status data and categorizes items. Examine this logic:

status_data = response.json().get('changes', [])

needs_commit = [c for c in status_data if c['workspaceChange'] != 'None']
needs_update = [c for c in status_data if c['remoteChange'] != 'None']

print(f'Items to commit: {len(needs_commit)}')
print(f'Items to update from Git: {len(needs_update)}')

What do the two lists 'needs_commit' and 'needs_update' represent respectively?

options.A: Items that failed deployment and items that succeeded deployment
options.B: Items with local workspace changes not yet committed to Git, and items with remote Git changes not yet pulled into the workspace
options.C: Items created today and items modified in the past 30 days
options.D: Items assigned to the Development stage and items assigned to the Test stage

questions.explanation: 'needs_commit' identifies items where workspaceChange is not 'None' — meaning local changes exist that haven't been pushed to Git (requires using 'Changes' in Source control). 'needs_update' identifies items where remoteChange is not 'None' — meaning Git has newer commits not yet pulled into the workspace (requires using 'Updates' in Source control).
